quiz_title: Python for DevOps — Class 3 Quiz: Building CLI Tools

questions.question:
A script runs as: python deploy.py prod 8080 --verbose
What does sys.argv contain?
questions.code:
import sys
print(sys.argv)
questions.explanation: sys.argv[0] is always the script NAME (matching C's argv[0]); it does NOT include the 'python' interpreter path, but it does include flags passed to the script. The user-supplied arguments start at sys.argv[1].

questions.question: The book recommends NOT building production CLI tools from raw sys.argv. What's the main reason?
options: Hand-rolled parsing silently ignores misspelled flags, doesn't generate help, and has no validation
questions.explanation: Hand-rolling argument parsing means: misspelled flags (--vrebose) are silently ignored, no auto-generated --help, no type checking, no required-argument enforcement, no choices validation, no error messages. argparse / click / fire exist precisely to solve this — use them.

questions.question: What does this print after the subprocess runs?
questions.code:
import subprocess
cp = subprocess.run(["echo", "hello"], capture_output=True, text=True)
print(repr(cp.stdout))
print(cp.returncode)
questions.explanation: subprocess.run with capture_output=True captures stdout into cp.stdout. text=True (or universal_newlines=True) decodes bytes to str. The captured output INCLUDES the trailing newline that echo adds — repr() shows it as '\n'. returncode is 0 for success.

questions.question: What happens here?
options: Nothing — ls just prints to stderr
questions.explanation: check=True makes subprocess.run RAISE CalledProcessError if the command exits non-zero. Without check=True you must inspect cp.returncode manually — easy to forget. ls returns exit code 2 when the path doesn't exist. Use check=True for fail-fast pipelines.

questions.question: Without check=True, what does this code do when /no_such_dir doesn't exist?
questions.code:
import subprocess
cp = subprocess.run(["ls", "/no_such_dir"], capture_output=True, text=True)
print(cp.returncode, len(cp.stderr) > 0)
options: Non-zero  True  (and your script keeps running)
questions.explanation: Without check=True, subprocess silently SUCCEEDS even when the command failed. cp.returncode will be non-zero and cp.stderr will contain the error message — but YOUR script keeps going as if all is well. This is the source of countless 'silently broken automation' bugs in production.

questions.question: What does subprocess.run return?
questions.code:
import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(["true"])
print(type(result).__name__)
options: CompletedProcess  (an object with returncode, stdout, stderr, args)
questions.explanation: subprocess.run returns a CompletedProcess object — a structured record of what happened: result.returncode (exit code), result.stdout, result.stderr (only populated if capture_output=True), result.args (the command). This is more robust than the older subprocess.call() which returns just the integer code.

questions.question: Why does every well-written Python CLI script end with this idiom?
questions.code:
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
options: Without it, calling main() would run when the module is imported elsewhere — not just when run directly
questions.explanation: When a Python file is imported, its top-level statements run. Without the __name__ guard, main() would execute every time someone imports your module — usually not what you want. The guard ensures main() runs ONLY when the file is executed directly (python script.py), not on import.

questions.question: What does this argparse code print for: python tool.py --verbose hello
questions.code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("message")
parser.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true")
args = parser.parse_args()
print(args.message, args.verbose, type(args.verbose).__name__)
options: Error — positional must come before flags
questions.explanation: action='store_true' makes the flag a BOOLEAN — True if present, False if absent. argparse handles flag/positional ordering automatically (you can put --verbose before or after 'hello'). args.message is a str (the positional value), args.verbose is a bool.

questions.question: What does this argparse code do when called with: python tool.py fly
questions.code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("mode", choices=["list", "sail"])
args = parser.parse_args()
options: args.mode is silently set to None
argparse prints an error and exits with code 2
questions.explanation: choices=[...] restricts allowed values. If the user passes something not in the list, argparse PRINTS A FRIENDLY ERROR and calls sys.exit(2) — your code never sees the bad input. This is much cleaner than manually validating after parse_args().

questions.question: What does this argparse subcommand setup yield for: python fleet.py ships
questions.code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd")
sub.add_parser("ships")
sub.add_parser("sailors")
args = parser.parse_args()
print(args.cmd)
options: Error: cmd is not a known argument
questions.explanation: add_subparsers(dest='cmd') stores the chosen subcommand name in args.cmd. So when the user runs 'fleet.py ships', args.cmd is 'ships'. Subcommands are how tools like git (git stash, git rebase) and kubectl (kubectl get, kubectl apply) structure their interfaces.

questions.question: Which library matches each design philosophy?
questions.code:
# Library A: Maximum control, separates UI code from business logic.
#            Most code lines. Stdlib.
#
# Library B: Function decorators tie flags directly to function parameters.
#            Less boilerplate.
#
# Library C: Auto-generates the CLI from your existing functions/classes
#            via introspection. Almost no extra code.
questions.explanation: argparse (stdlib) is verbose but maximally explicit. click uses decorators (@click.command, @click.option) — middle ground. fire turns ANY function or class into a CLI via introspection — fastest to write, least control. The book recommends click for most cases.

questions.question: What does this click setup expose to the command line?
options: A function callable from Python only
A CLI tool: ./script.py --name Alice prints 'Hello, Alice'; ./script.py prints 'Hello, world'
questions.explanation: click.command() turns greet() into a CLI entry point. click.option('--name', default='world') links the --name flag DIRECTLY to the name parameter; if not given, default is used. Calling greet() at the bottom invokes click's parser. Auto-generates --help too.

questions.question: Which fire usage exposes BOTH a 'sail' command and a 'list' command on the CLI?
options: B — fire.Fire(Class) exposes each method as a subcommand
questions.explanation: fire.Fire(callable) on a single function exposes only that function. Pass a CLASS and fire introspects its methods, exposing each as a subcommand: 'script.py sail' calls Ships().sail(). This is fire's signature feature — zero decorator boilerplate.

questions.question: What's the simplest way for a Python program to discover and load 'plug-in' modules whose names share a prefix?
options: You must restart Python for new plug-ins to be visible
pkgutil.iter_modules() lists installed modules; importlib.import_module() loads one by name
Plug-ins must be hardcoded — there's no discovery API
questions.explanation: pkgutil.iter_modules() yields (finder, name, ispkg) for every importable module on sys.path. Filter by a known prefix (the plug-in convention), then importlib.import_module(name) loads each one. Now you can call any agreed-upon API on the loaded module (here, .run()).

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: sys.version_info is a named tuple: (major, minor, micro, releaselevel, serial). On Python 3.12: major=3 (not <3), minor=12 (not <6), so the else branch runs. SREs use this pattern to gate features that need newer Python (f-strings need 3.6, walrus needs 3.8, etc.).

questions.question: What is sys.platform on a typical Ubuntu server?
questions.code:
import sys
print(sys.platform)
questions.explanation: sys.platform returns 'linux' for any Linux distro (Ubuntu, RHEL, Alpine — all 'linux'). Other values: 'darwin' (macOS), 'win32' (Windows, even 64-bit), 'freebsd*'. Use this to fork OS-specific behavior — but prefer the os module's portable functions when possible.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.code:
import os
os.environ["DEPLOY_TAG"] = "v2.3.1"
import subprocess
cp = subprocess.run(["sh", "-c", "echo $DEPLOY_TAG"], capture_output=True, text=True)
print(cp.stdout.strip())
questions.explanation: os.environ assignments propagate to subprocesses spawned BY this Python process — that's a key DevOps technique. The subshell sees DEPLOY_TAG=v2.3.1 and echoes it. NOTE: os.environ changes do NOT affect the parent shell that launched Python; they're visible only to children.

questions.question: What's wrong with this argparse setup?
questions.code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("hostname")  # required
args = parser.parse_args()  # called with no arguments
print(args.hostname)
options: argparse prints a 'required' error and calls sys.exit(2)
questions.explanation: Positional arguments are REQUIRED by default. If missing, argparse prints 'usage: ... error: the following arguments are required: hostname' and calls sys.exit(2). Your code never executes. To make it optional, use nargs='?' with a default, or convert to a flag like --hostname.

questions.question: What does fire's --interactive flag do?
questions.code: $ ./fleet.py sailors greet "Alice" -- --interactive
options: Drops you into an IPython REPL with the function's local objects available — great for debugging
questions.explanation: fire's --interactive flag (after the -- separator) opens an IPython shell with all of your script's objects (classes, functions, the result of the call) bound as variables. You can poke at internal state without rewriting any code — a powerful debugging shortcut unique to fire.

questions.question: An automation script needs to run 'kubectl get pods' and capture both stdout and stderr without crashing if kubectl exits non-zero. What's the right call?
questions.code:
# Option A
subprocess.run(["kubectl", "get", "pods"], check=True)

# Option B
subprocess.run(["kubectl", "get", "pods"], capture_output=True, text=True)

# Option C
subprocess.run(["kubectl", "get", "pods"], capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
options: B — captures output, no exception on failure (you inspect cp.returncode)
C — captures output, raises on failure (you can't read stderr)
Both B and C; choose based on whether you want fail-fast or graceful handling
questions.explanation: Both B and C are valid; pick by intent. Use C (with check=True) when failure should ABORT the script — the exception's e.stderr still has the captured output. Use B (no check) when you want to inspect cp.returncode and react gracefully. Option A doesn't capture anything — kubectl's output streams straight to YOUR terminal, useless for automation.
